articles.title: Gladiator Giant Departs BBC Show After Three Seasons
articles.summary: Jamie Bigg, known as Giant on the BBC show Gladiators, has announced his departure after three seasons. Bigg shared the news on Instagram, stating that being a Gladiator was one of the greatest honors of his life. However, he faced a choice that did not align with his values, leading to his exit. The show's spokesperson thanked him for his contributions and wished him well. Bigg, a former professional bodybuilder and firefighter from Ilkeston, stands 6ft 5in tall with 52cm biceps and consumes 10,000 calories daily to maintain his physique. He joined Gladiators when it returned in 2024. In January, he dropped his double-barrelled surname Christian-Johal after his divorce and ADHD diagnosis. Bigg emphasized that his departure was not his decision but a result of a values conflict. He expressed gratitude to fans and said this is a new beginning.

articles.title: BBC Radio 1 Warns Against Ticket Touting for Big Weekend
articles.summary: BBC Radio 1 has warned fans not to buy tickets for its Big Weekend festival from touts or unauthorized sellers. Entry to the three-day event starts at £45, but tickets have been seen online for more than 12 times the face value, up to £622. Music industry group FanFair Alliance said this shows fans being exploited and called for a new law to cap resale prices. The government said it was 'committed' to delivering the ban, but resellers StubHub and Viagogo argued that a cap could drive people to unregulated websites, increasing fraud risk and driving prices higher. Radio 1 stated that tickets bought from touts may be voided, and holders could be refused entry. The festival is primarily for Sunderland residents, with 90% of tickets allocated locally. The official seller is Ticketmaster, which limits customers to two tickets per day and requires resales on its dedicated site. FanFair Alliance found that two-thirds of tickets were sold by traders, not individuals, and some sellers offered up to 30 tickets. Newsbeat's checks found similar overpriced listings.

articles.title: How Falling Share Prices Impact Your Pension
articles.summary: The deputy governor of the Bank of England recently warned that share prices might be too high and could fall. This matters because many people's pensions are invested in the stock market. When share prices drop, the value of pension savings can decrease. In the UK, millions of people have defined contribution pensions, where the final amount depends on investment performance. These pensions are often automatically enrolled through employers, who contribute money alongside the employee. The government also adds tax relief. While falling markets can be worrying, experts emphasize that pensions are long-term investments. History shows that markets recover over time, so making hasty decisions can harm long-term growth. It's important to stay informed but not panic, as short-term fluctuations are normal. Understanding this connection helps people make better financial decisions for their future.

articles.title: Paul Weller: The Modfather Embraces Family and New Music
articles.summary: Paul Weller, the legendary frontman of The Jam and a key figure in the mod revival, is set to release a new album titled 'Weller At The BBC Volume 2'. The album features live performances of his classic hits and covers of other artists' songs, including Billie Eilish's 'What Was I Made For?' from the Barbie movie soundtrack. Weller, now 67, says he was inspired to cover the song because his youngest daughter loves it. He praises Eilish's songwriting, calling the melody beautiful and the lyrics clever. Weller has also quit drinking, which he says has made him more appreciative of music. Reflecting on his early career, he recalls his first gig at Woking Working Men's Club, where only 10 people attended, most of whom complained about the noise until after bingo. Today, he has a studio in Ripley where he creates music. Weller has two grandchildren and downplays being a 'cool granddad', instead saying he is just like any other grandparent. He enjoys sharing music with his eight children, but emphasizes that the exchange is mutual.

articles.title: Long-Lost Morecambe and Wise Episode Rediscovered and Set to Air
articles.summary: A missing episode of the iconic British comedy duo Morecambe and Wise, originally broadcast on September 16, 1968, has been recovered from a private collection. The episode is part of their return to the BBC after a stint with ATV. The copy was preserved by a deceased industry professional. The BBC plans to broadcast it in the coming months. Professor Justin Smith, chair of trustees at Film is Fabulous! (FIF), the charitable trust that tracked it down, describes it as fast-paced and witty, featuring trademark sketches like a balloon dancer and a nudist camp scene that reflect the 'permissive society' of the era. Smith notes that the duo were in their prime. The recording is in black and white, but FIF believes it can be restored to original quality. This discovery adds to the legacy of Morecambe and Wise, who remain beloved in British comedy.

articles.title: US Accuses China of Industrial-Scale AI Theft
articles.summary: The White House has issued a memo accusing foreign entities, primarily based in China, of conducting industrial-scale campaigns to steal US artificial intelligence (AI) technology. Michael Kratsios, Director of Science and Technology Policy, stated that these entities use a technique called 'distilling' to copy AI models. Distillation involves operating thousands of accounts to appear as normal users, then attempting to 'jailbreak' the AI to extract proprietary information. The memo outlines four unspecified actions to combat this exploitation. A representative from China's US embassy countered the accusations, claiming China's AI development is a result of its own efforts and international cooperation, and criticized US suppression of Chinese companies. Major AI firms like OpenAI and Anthropic have reported similar distillation activities. The memo emphasizes that as detection methods improve, foreign entities relying on stolen technology will produce unreliable models.
